terms.definition: A shelter design resembling the letter 'A', providing protection on two sides with a ridge pole at the top. Good for shedding rain and snow.
The area of the body between the chest and the pelvis, containing the stomach, intestines, liver, and other vital organs.
A superficial wound where the top layers of skin are scraped or worn away by friction. Road rash is a common example.
A pocket of pus that forms in tissue, usually caused by bacterial infection. Common in untreated wounds and dental infections.
A pain reliever and fever reducer that does not reduce inflammation. Known as paracetamol outside the US. Brand name: Tylenol.
The nut of an oak tree. Edible after leaching out bitter tannins by soaking in water. A high-calorie survival food source.
To give or apply a treatment, medication, or remedy to a patient.
A cutting tool with the blade set perpendicular to the handle, used for shaping wood. Like an axe but cuts across the grain.
A naturally occurring chemical compound in plants that can have strong effects on the body. Some are medicinal (caffeine, quinine), others are toxic (nicotine, strychnine).
A substance that triggers an allergic reaction in sensitive individuals. Common allergens include insect venom, foods, pollen, and plant oils.
Relating to high mountain environments above the tree line, characterized by cold temperatures, thin air, and exposure to wind and UV radiation.
Relating to the immediate surrounding conditions. Ambient temperature is the air temperature of the environment.
Acute Mountain Sickness — the mildest form of altitude illness, characterized by headache, nausea, fatigue, and dizziness above 8,000 feet.
A medication that relieves pain without causing loss of consciousness. Examples: ibuprofen, acetaminophen, aspirin.

A severe, potentially life-threatening allergic reaction that occurs within seconds or minutes of exposure to an allergen. Symptoms include swelling, hives, breathing difficulty, and a drop in blood pressure.
The front side of the body. An anterior wound is on the front.
A medication that blocks the effects of histamine, reducing allergic symptoms like swelling, itching, and hives. Examples: Benadryl, Zyrtec.
A chemical substance applied to skin or wounds to kill or prevent the growth of harmful microorganisms. Examples: iodine, alcohol, hydrogen peroxide.
The region around the North Pole, characterized by extreme cold, permafrost, and polar conditions. Arctic survival requires specialized skills.
Extremely dry with very little rainfall. Arid environments (deserts) present challenges of heat, dehydration, and sun exposure.
Relating to arteries — the blood vessels carrying oxygen-rich blood from the heart. Arterial bleeding is bright red and spurts with each heartbeat.
Death or loss of consciousness caused by being unable to breathe. Can result from choking, drowning, smoke inhalation, or chest compression.
To accidentally inhale food, liquid, or vomit into the lungs. Also: to draw fluid out of a body cavity using suction.
To evaluate a patient's condition through observation, questioning, and physical examination to determine appropriate treatment.
Alert, Voice responsive, Pain responsive, Unresponsive — a quick scale for assessing a patient's level of consciousness.
A pointed tool used for piercing holes in leather, wood, or other materials. Can be improvised from bone, thorns, or sharpened sticks.
A horizontal angle measured clockwise from north. Used in navigation to express direction, equivalent to bearing in most contexts.
A fast-growing woody grass found in tropical and subtropical regions. Extremely versatile in survival: used for shelter, water containers, tools, weapons, and food (young shoots are edible).
The outer protective covering of a tree trunk and branches. Inner bark (cambium) of many trees is edible. Outer bark is used for containers, cordage, and fire starting.
Physical damage to body tissues caused by a difference in pressure between a gas space inside the body and the surrounding fluid or air.
A thick stick used to strike the spine of a knife to split wood (batoning). A technique for processing firewood without an axe.
The direction you want to travel, expressed in degrees (0-360). 'Heading' is the direction you are currently facing.
To secure a rope around a fixed object (tree, rock, cleat) to control or stop movement. Used in climbing and rescue.
A raised bank or barrier of earth. Used in shelter construction and water management to redirect flow.
A curved section or loop in a rope between the two ends. Many knots are tied 'in the bight,' meaning without needing access to the rope ends.
A large ecological area defined by its climate, soil, and dominant vegetation. Examples: tundra, tropical rainforest, desert, boreal forest.
The outer bark of birch trees, which is thin, papery, and contains flammable oils. Excellent fire-starting tinder even when damp.
A temporary camp without tents or cover, used especially by soldiers or mountaineers. Also refers to a 'Bivy Sack', a lightweight waterproof cover for a sleeping bag.
A venomous spider identified by a red hourglass marking on its abdomen. Its bite causes severe muscle pain and cramping.
Injury caused by a forceful impact from a dull object, without penetrating the skin. Can cause internal bleeding, organ damage, and fractures.
Relating to the northern forests of cold regions, characterized by coniferous trees (spruce, pine, fir). Also called taiga.
A friction-based fire-starting method using a spindle, fireboard, bearing block, and bow to generate an ember through rotational friction.
A knot that creates a fixed loop at the end of a rope. Known as the 'king of knots' because it's secure, easy to tie, and easy to untie even after loading.
An abnormally slow heart rate, usually defined as fewer than 60 beats per minute.
A strong saltwater solution used to preserve food. Brining draws moisture out of meat and creates an inhospitable environment for bacteria.
A venomous spider identified by a violin-shaped marking on its back. Its bite can cause necrotic (tissue-destroying) wounds.
A round underground storage organ of a plant (like an onion). Wild onion and garlic bulbs are edible and found in many regions.
A plant with large leaves and sticky burrs. Its root is edible when cooked — tastes similar to potato. Used in herbal medicine for skin conditions.
A hidden store of supplies or food placed in advance along a route or at a survival location for later retrieval.
A man-made pile of stones used as a trail marker or navigational aid in wilderness areas.
The thin layer of living tissue between tree bark and wood. Edible in many tree species (pine, birch, maple) and a survival food source.
The uppermost layer of branches and leaves in a forest, forming a natural roof. Dense canopy reduces ground-level rainfall and sunlight.
A portable water container, typically made of metal or plastic, carried for drinking water in the field.
The smallest blood vessels connecting arteries to veins. Capillary bleeding (scrapes, minor cuts) is slow, oozing, and stops on its own.
A metal loop with a spring-loaded gate, used to quickly and reversibly connect ropes, harnesses, and equipment.
The major artery on each side of the neck supplying blood to the brain. The carotid pulse is checked to assess circulation.
An area from which rainfall is collected. In survival, a rain catchment is a system of tarps or containers to collect drinking water.
A small hole dug for the disposal of human waste in the wilderness to prevent contamination of water sources and spread of disease.
A tall wetland plant (Typha) with a distinctive brown cigar-shaped seed head. Nearly every part is edible: roots, shoots, pollen, and young flower stalks.
A bacterial skin infection that causes redness, swelling, warmth, and pain. Can spread rapidly and become life-threatening if untreated.
Partially burned organic material that catches sparks easily. Charred cotton (char cloth) is a traditional fire-starting aid.
Cotton fabric that has been charred (heated without oxygen) so it ignites from a single spark. Used as fire-starting tinder.
A fine-grained sedimentary rock similar to flint. Produces sparks when struck and can be shaped into cutting tools.
A plant with bright blue flowers. The roasted root is used as a coffee substitute. Young leaves are edible as salad greens.
The collarbone — a thin bone connecting the shoulder blade to the sternum. One of the most commonly broken bones.
A dried flower bud used as a spice, with strong natural analgesic (pain-killing) properties. Clove oil applied to a tooth relieves dental pain.
A simple knot used to attach a rope to a post or pole. Quick to tie but can slip under varying loads.
The chemical process of burning, where a substance reacts with oxygen to produce heat and light. Requires fuel, heat, and oxygen (fire triangle).
A painful condition caused by pressure buildup within muscles. Can lead to tissue death if not treated. Often follows crush injuries or tight splinting.
To apply firm, steady pressure to a wound or injury. A cold compress reduces swelling; a warm compress increases blood flow.
A mild traumatic brain injury caused by a blow to the head. Symptoms include confusion, headache, dizziness, and sometimes loss of consciousness.
Water droplets that form when warm, moist air meets a cooler surface. Important in water collection and shelter management.
Heat transfer through direct contact. Sitting on cold ground causes conductive heat loss — ground insulation prevents this.
To tighten or squeeze. A tourniquet constricts blood flow. Constriction bands are used for some envenomations.
A flat, wide elastic band wrapped above a snakebite to slow the spread of venom through the lymphatic system. NOT a tourniquet — does not stop blood flow.
Able to spread from person to person through direct or indirect contact. Contagious diseases require isolation and hygiene precautions.
Made impure or unsafe by contact with harmful substances or organisms. Contaminated water, wounds, or food can cause illness.
A line on a topographic map connecting points of equal elevation. Closely spaced lines indicate steep terrain; widely spaced indicate flat ground.
A bruise — an injury where blood vessels under the skin are damaged without breaking the skin surface.
Heat transfer through the movement of fluids (air or water). Wind causes convective heat loss from the body.
A highly venomous snake with distinctive red, yellow, and black bands. Remembered by the rhyme: 'Red touches yellow, kill a fellow.' Has neurotoxic venom.
Rope, string, or cord made from natural or synthetic fibers. In survival, can be made from plant fibers, bark, or animal sinew.
Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation. A lifesaving technique useful in many emergencies, including a heart attack or near drowning, in which someone's breathing or heartbeat has stopped.
A triangular bandage folded into a strip, used for binding splints, making slings, or applying pressure to wounds.
A black, tar-like substance that builds up in chimneys and flues from incomplete combustion of wood. Highly flammable — a chimney fire hazard.
A grinding or crackling sensation felt or heard when fractured bone ends rub together, or air trapped in tissues.
A waterborne parasite resistant to iodine and chlorine treatment. Removed by filtration (pore size ≤1 micron) or UV treatment.
A below-ground fire pit connected to an air tunnel, producing a smokeless, wind-resistant fire with efficient heat for cooking.
A common plant with yellow flowers and jagged leaves. Entirely edible: leaves for salad, roots roasted as coffee substitute, flowers for tea. High in vitamins A, C, K.
Navigating by tracking direction and distance traveled from a known position, without celestial or electronic aids.
A trap that kills game by dropping a heavy weight (rock or log) when a trigger mechanism is disturbed by the animal.
Causing severe weakness or impairment. A debilitating injury prevents normal function and self-rescue.
The removal of dead, damaged, or infected tissue from a wound to promote healing and prevent infection.
Scattered remains of something broken or destroyed. In shelter building, loose natural materials (leaves, branches, bark) used for insulation and covering.
An insulated shelter built by piling leaves, branches, and other forest debris over a framework of sticks. Highly effective for cold weather survival.
The angular difference between true north (geographic) and magnetic north (where the compass needle points). It varies by location.
A preparation made by boiling plant material (bark, roots, seeds) in water to extract its active compounds. Stronger than an infusion.
A harmful reduction in the amount of water in the body. Symptoms include thirst, dark urine, and fatigue. Severe dehydration can lead to confusion and death.
To reduce the number of harmful microorganisms to a safe level. Less thorough than sterilizing but adequate for most field situations.
A joint injury where bones are forced out of their normal alignment. Causes intense pain, swelling, and inability to move the joint.
Farther from the center of the body or point of attachment. The fingers are distal to the elbow.
Relating to the back or upper surface. The top of the hand is the dorsal side.
To apply a sterile covering (dressing) to a wound. A wound dressing protects from contamination and absorbs fluids.
A circular current in water, formed where the main current meets an obstruction. Can trap swimmers but also indicates calmer water behind obstacles.
Swelling caused by excess fluid trapped in the body's tissues. Can indicate injury, allergic reaction, or systemic illness.
Safe to eat. In survival, edible plants are those tested or known to be non-toxic for human consumption.
Minerals in blood and body fluids that carry an electric charge (sodium, potassium, chloride, bicarbonate). Essential for muscle and nerve function.
To raise a body part above the level of the heart to reduce swelling and slow bleeding. A key component of RICE treatment.
A small, glowing piece of combustible material (like charred wood) that can be nursed into flame when placed in a tinder bundle.
A thick layer of hot coals remaining after a fire burns down. Used for cooking, warmth, and maintaining fire overnight.
The process by which venom is injected into another organism by a bite or sting from a venomous animal.
A hormone and medication also known as adrenaline. Used to treat severe allergic reactions, cardiac arrest, and severe asthma attacks.
A brand-name auto-injector that delivers a measured dose of epinephrine (adrenaline) to treat severe allergic reactions (anaphylaxis).
Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon — a maritime distress beacon that alerts search and rescue services via satellite when activated.
The tube connecting the throat to the stomach. Food passes through the esophagus; it sits behind the trachea.
Where a river meets the sea, creating a mix of fresh and salt water. Rich in food resources but water is not potable without desalination.
To move a patient from a dangerous location to a place where they can receive medical care. Also: to clear an area of people.
The process of liquid becoming vapor, which removes heat. Sweating cools the body through evaporation, but also causes fluid loss.
To uncover or reveal a wound or injury for examination. In trauma assessment, 'expose' means removing clothing to check for hidden injuries.
A limb of the body — an arm or leg. 'Upper extremity' = arm; 'lower extremity' = leg.
To free someone from a trapped or confined situation, such as a collapsed structure, vehicle wreck, or avalanche debris.
Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call emergency services — a mnemonic for stroke recognition.
Resinous heartwood from dead pine trees, naturally saturated with flammable resin. Lights easily even when wet. Also called lighter wood.
The thighbone — the longest and strongest bone in the human body. A femur fracture can cause life-threatening internal bleeding.
Short for Ferrocerium Rod. A man-made metallic material that produces very hot sparks (3,000°C / 5,400°F) when scraped with a sharp edge.
The arrangement of tinder, kindling, and fuel for starting a fire. Common lays include teepee, log cabin, lean-to, and star fire.
A wall of logs or rocks built behind a fire to reflect heat toward a shelter or sleeping area, increasing warmth efficiency.
The process of chipping thin pieces off a stone to create a sharp edge. Used in making stone knives, arrowheads, and scrapers.
The lowest temperature at which a substance produces enough vapor to ignite when exposed to a spark or flame.
A hard, dark gray stone (a type of quartz) that produces sparks when struck against steel. Used for fire-starting since prehistoric times.
A traditional fire-starting method using a piece of high-carbon steel struck against flint to produce sparks that ignite charcloth or tinder.
To wash out a wound, eye, or area with a large volume of clean water. Used for chemical burns, contaminated wounds, and eye injuries.
To search for and collect wild food (plants, berries, nuts, insects). A critical survival skill when food supplies are unavailable.
A break or crack in a bone. Types include simple (closed), compound (open — bone pierces skin), hairline, and comminuted (shattered).
A large, divided leaf, especially of a fern or palm. Palm fronds are used for thatching shelters; some fern fiddleheads are edible.
Injury to body tissues caused by exposure to extreme cold, typically affecting the nose, fingers, or toes.
Larger logs or wood used to sustain a fire. Ranges from wrist-thick branches to large logs depending on the fire's purpose.
A very strong wind (39-54 mph / 63-87 km/h). Gale-force winds can destroy shelters and make travel dangerous.
Death and decay of body tissue, usually caused by a lack of blood supply. Can be wet (infected) or dry.
A standard measure. Wire gauge measures thickness; rain gauge measures precipitation. In medical context, the diameter of a needle.
Glasgow Coma Scale — a neurological scale used to objectively record a patient's level of consciousness, scored 3-15.
A biological classification grouping of closely related species. For example, the genus Quercus includes all oak trees.
A waterborne parasite that causes severe diarrhea, cramps, and dehydration. Found in untreated wilderness water sources worldwide.
Coarse particles of stone or sand. In water filtration, grit layers help remove larger suspended particles.
Material placed between the body and the ground to prevent conductive heat loss. Critical in cold environments — the ground draws heat 25x faster than air.
A large visual symbol placed on the ground to communicate with aircraft. Common signals include V (need assistance), X (need medical help), and arrows.
The larval stage of an insect, typically found in rotting wood or soil. High in protein and fat. A legitimate survival food source.
The natural environment where a particular species of plant or animal lives and grows. Knowing habitats helps locate food and predict animal behavior.
High Altitude Cerebral Edema — a severe and potentially fatal condition where the brain swells with fluid at high altitude.
The most primitive friction fire method, using only a spindle and fireboard rotated between the palms. Requires very dry materials.
High Altitude Pulmonary Edema — a life-threatening accumulation of fluid in the lungs occurring at high altitudes, typically above 8,000 feet.
An emergency technique used to dislodge a foreign object from a choking person's airway using abdominal thrusts.
Severe, uncontrolled bleeding. Can be external (visible) or internal (hidden). Life-threatening — a person can bleed out in minutes.
A substance that promotes blood clotting and stops bleeding. Examples include QuikClot, Celox, and improvised alternatives like cobwebs.
The amount of water vapor in the air. High humidity slows sweat evaporation, reducing the body's cooling ability and increasing heat illness risk.
The dry outer covering of a seed, fruit, or nut. Coconut husk fiber (coir) is used for cordage; corn husks for wrapping food.
A condition where the body's temperature is elevated beyond normal due to failed thermoregulation. Heat exhaustion and heatstroke are forms of hyperthermia.
Dangerously low blood sugar levels causing shakiness, confusion, dizziness, and potentially unconsciousness. Treated with sugar intake.
A medical emergency that occurs when your body loses heat faster than it can produce it, causing a dangerously low body temperature (below 95°F / 35°C).
Relating to or suffering from hypothermia. A hypothermic patient has a dangerously low core body temperature.
A multi-layer insulation system using vapor barriers, insulation, and wind protection to rewarm a hypothermic patient in the field.
A non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) used to reduce pain, fever, and inflammation. Common brand names: Advil, Motrin.
To prevent a body part from moving, typically using a splint, sling, or bandage. Critical for fractures and spinal injuries to prevent further damage.
To create or perform something using whatever materials are immediately available, without advance planning or specialized tools.
Made from whatever materials are available rather than purpose-built equipment. A key survival concept — adapting tools from the environment.
A clean, deliberate cut made with a sharp instrument. Distinguished from a laceration which has irregular, torn edges.
The invasion and multiplication of harmful microorganisms (bacteria, viruses, fungi) in body tissue. Signs include redness, swelling, warmth, pus, and fever.
The body's response to injury or infection, characterized by redness, swelling, heat, and pain. A protective mechanism that can become harmful if excessive.
A preparation made by steeping plant material (leaves, flowers) in hot water. Similar to tea. Used for medicinal purposes in the field.
To add material between surfaces to prevent heat transfer. In survival, insulation between the body and ground/air is critical for warmth.
A chemical element used to purify water by killing bacteria, viruses, and parasites. Added as drops or tablets. Leaves a slight taste.
To flush a wound, eye, or body cavity with a stream of water or saline solution to clean out debris, chemicals, or contaminants.
Meat that has been cut into thin strips and dried (by sun, smoke, or salt) to preserve it. Lightweight, high-protein survival food.
Small twigs and sticks (pencil-sized) used to transition a fire from tinder to larger fuel logs.
A prepared collection of small, dry sticks organized for efficient fire starting. Pre-arranged so flame can spread easily from tinder.
The art of shaping stone (flint, obsidian, chert) into tools by controlled fracturing. An ancient skill for creating cutting edges.
A deep cut or tear in the skin or flesh, often caused by sharp objects. Distinguished from incisions by their irregular edges.
A recognizable natural or man-made feature used as a reference point for navigation. Mountains, rivers, towers, and distinctive trees serve as landmarks.
The immature form of an insect before metamorphosis. Mealworms, beetle grubs, and fly larvae are protein-rich emergency food sources.
A technique for binding poles or sticks together using rope or cordage to build frameworks, shelters, rafts, and tools.
A communal toilet facility in a camp or bivouac area.
The process of soaking a substance in water to remove unwanted chemicals. Acorns must be leached to remove bitter, toxic tannins before eating.
A simple shelter usually leaning against a solid object (like a fallen tree or rock) with an open front and a sloping roof.
The side sheltered from the wind. Building shelter on the leeward side of a hill or rock provides wind protection.
Using a rigid bar (lever) pivoting on a fixed point (fulcrum) to multiply force. Essential for moving heavy objects in rescue and construction.
A composite organism of fungus and algae growing on rocks and trees. Some species are edible (rock tripe), others are toxic. A slow-growing food of last resort.
A band of tough, fibrous tissue connecting bones at a joint. Ligaments stabilize joints and can be damaged by sprains.
A stretcher or portable platform used to carry an injured person who cannot walk. Can be improvised from poles and clothing.
Capable of supporting weight or force without breaking. A load-bearing knot must hold securely under tension.
The direction a compass needle points — toward the Earth's magnetic pole, which differs from true geographic north by a variable amount (declination).
The soft, fatty tissue inside bones. Extremely calorie-dense and nutritious. Extracted by splitting bones and scooping or boiling.
A type of wetland with grassy vegetation, saturated with water. Marshes are rich in edible plants (cattails) but present navigation and shelter challenges.
Medical evacuation — transporting an injured or ill person to a medical facility, typically by helicopter or ambulance.
To continuously observe and check a patient's vital signs and condition over time for changes.
A tall plant with large, fuzzy leaves and a yellow flower spike. Leaves used as improvised wound padding, toilet paper, and insulation. Tea used for respiratory issues.
An ultra-thin, lightweight sheet made of heat-reflective metallic coating. Reflects up to 90% of radiated body heat. Also called a space blanket or emergency blanket.
The death of body tissue. Often appears as blackened, dead skin. Can result from severe frostbite, venom, infection, or loss of blood supply.
A specialized stinging cell found in jellyfish tentacles. When triggered, it injects venom through a microscopic barbed thread.
A plant with stinging hairs that cause a burning rash on contact. Once boiled or dried, nettles are highly nutritious — rich in iron, calcium, and vitamins.
A cognitive bias causing people to underestimate the likelihood and impact of a disaster, leading to inadequate preparation and delayed response.
A V-shaped cut or indentation. In fire-starting, the notch in a fireboard collects hot dust. In shelter building, notches join logs together.
Volcanic glass — extremely hard and can be knapped into razor-sharp edges. Used historically for cutting tools and arrowheads.
An airtight, waterproof wound covering used to seal chest wounds (sucking chest wounds) and prevent air from entering the pleural space.
The internal organs of an animal (heart, liver, kidneys, intestines). Highly nutritious and should not be discarded in a survival situation.
Oral Rehydration Solution — a precise mixture of water, salt, and sugar used to treat dehydration, especially from diarrhea or vomiting.
A method of measuring distance traveled on foot by counting steps. A known number of paces equals a specific distance (commonly 100 meters).
To examine by touch, especially as part of a medical assessment. Feeling for broken bones, swelling, tenderness, or abnormal masses.
Type III 550 parachute cord — a lightweight nylon rope originally used in parachute suspension lines. Has a minimum breaking strength of 550 pounds.
An organism that causes disease, such as bacteria, viruses, or parasites. Water purification aims to remove or kill these.
A concentrated, long-lasting food made from dried meat, rendered fat, and sometimes dried berries. A traditional survival ration.
Injury where an object pierces the skin and enters body tissue. Stab wounds, gunshot wounds, and impalement are penetrating injuries.
Striking one object against another. In fire-starting, striking steel against flint to produce sparks. In knapping, shaping stone tools by striking.
Ground that remains frozen for two or more consecutive years. Found in arctic and subarctic regions. Limits digging for shelter, latrines, and graves.
A tea brewed from fresh pine needles, rich in vitamin C. Used in survival to prevent scurvy and as a warm, immune-boosting drink.
A family of venomous snakes with heat-sensing pits between their eyes and nostrils. Includes rattlesnakes, copperheads, and cottonmouths. Found throughout the Americas.
The soft, spongy tissue in the center of stems and branches. Elderberry pith is used for tinder; cattail pith is edible.
A common broadleaf weed (Plantago major), NOT the banana-like fruit. The leaves can be chewed and applied to insect stings, bites, and minor wounds as a field remedy.
Personal Locator Beacon — a device that transmits a distress signal on 406 MHz to search and rescue satellites, providing your GPS location.
A collapsed lung caused by air leaking into the space between the lung and chest wall. Can result from a penetrating chest wound.
The back side of the body. A posterior wound is on the back.
Water that is safe to drink or to use for food preparation.
Potassium-rich ash from burned hardwood. Used in soap making, fertilizer, and water purification in survival contexts.
A soft, moist mass of plant material, clay, or other substance applied to the body to relieve soreness, inflammation, or draw out infection.
Any form of water falling from the atmosphere — rain, snow, sleet, hail. Important for water collection and shelter planning.
A bandage applied firmly to a wound to maintain constant pressure and control bleeding. Also called a compression bandage.
A friction hitch used to attach a cord to a rope, gripping when loaded and sliding when unloaded. Used in climbing and rescue.
The rhythmic expansion of an artery as the heart pumps blood. Checked at the wrist (radial) or neck (carotid) to assess heart rate and circulation.
A small device clipped to a fingertip that measures blood oxygen saturation (SpO2) and pulse rate.
Soft, rotten, dry wood that crumbles easily. Excellent for catching sparks and holding an ember. Found in dead standing trees.
To remove contaminants, pathogens, or impurities from water to make it safe for drinking. Methods include boiling, chemical treatment, filtering, and UV.
A shelter made by hollowing out a mound of settled snow. Simpler to build than an igloo and provides excellent cold weather protection.
A fatal viral infection transmitted through the bite of an infected animal. Once symptoms appear, it is almost always fatal.
Relating to the radius bone in the forearm, or the radial artery at the wrist where a pulse is commonly checked.
Heat transfer through electromagnetic waves. The body radiates heat to cooler surroundings. Reflective blankets reduce radiated heat loss.
To descend a cliff or steep surface by sliding down a rope in a controlled manner. Also called abseiling.
A deep, narrow valley with steep sides, typically formed by water erosion. Dangerous in flash flood zones but can provide shelter from wind.
A stable side-lying position used to maintain an unconscious but breathing person's open airway and prevent aspiration of vomit.
A medicinal mushroom (Ganoderma) that grows on dead hardwood trees. Used in traditional medicine for immune support. Has a distinctive kidney-shaped, lacquered appearance.
To melt down animal fat by heating it slowly. Rendered fat (tallow or lard) is used for cooking, waterproofing, candles, and making pemmican.
Sticky substance produced by trees, especially conifers. Flammable, waterproof, and useful as glue, sealant, and fire starter.
An underground horizontal stem that sends out roots and shoots. Cattail, ginger, and many ferns spread via rhizomes. Often edible.
Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation — a standard first aid protocol for treating soft tissue injuries like sprains and strains.
The top edge of a hill, mountain, or roof where two slopes meet. In shelter building, the ridgeline is the main horizontal support.
The horizontal beam at the top of a shelter or roof that supports the rafters. The structural backbone of an A-frame or lean-to.
A survival guideline: 3 minutes without air, 3 hours without shelter (in harsh conditions), 3 days without water, 3 weeks without food.
A solution of salt (sodium chloride) dissolved in water, matching the body's natural salt concentration. Used for wound irrigation and IV fluids.
A lightweight, highly moldable padded aluminum splint that can be shaped to immobilize almost any bone or joint.
The fluid inside a plant that carries water and nutrients. Maple and birch sap can be drunk directly or boiled into syrup.
Small loose rocks covering a mountain slope. Difficult and dangerous to traverse — rocks slide underfoot.
A place where groundwater slowly oozes to the surface. Found at the base of hills, in shady areas, or where vegetation is unusually green.
A life-threatening condition where the body's response to infection damages its own tissues and organs. Can progress rapidly from a wound infection.
The outer covering of a rope that protects the inner core strands from abrasion and UV damage. Paracord has a braided nylon sheath.
A knot used to shorten a rope without cutting it, or to bypass a damaged section. Useful but can slip if not under constant tension.
A structure that provides protection from the elements (wind, rain, sun, snow) and helps maintain body temperature.

A critical condition brought on by a sudden drop in blood flow through the body. The circulatory system fails to maintain adequate blood flow, sharply curtailing the delivery of oxygen and nutrients to vital organs.
A reflective device used to signal rescue aircraft or distant observers by reflecting sunlight. Effective at distances up to 50 miles.
A device used to support and immobilize an injured arm, typically made from a triangular bandage or improvised from clothing.
Using wood smoke to preserve and flavor food. Smoking creates an acidic coating that inhibits bacterial growth. Hot smoking cooks; cold smoking preserves.
A trap made from wire, cord, or natural fiber designed to catch small game by tightening around the animal as it passes through.
A shelter dug into deep snow, providing insulation and protection from wind. Interior temperature stays near 32°F/0°C regardless of outside temperature.
A device that uses solar energy to evaporate water (from purity or vegetation) and collect the condensate as clean drinking water.
A tangy, edible wild green plant high in vitamin C. Identify by arrow-shaped leaves. Eat in moderation — high oxalic acid content.
A universally recognized distress signal: three short, three long, three short (· · · — — — · · ·) in Morse code, or simply the letters 'SOS'.
A group of organisms that can reproduce with each other. The most specific level of biological classification (e.g., Achillea millefolium = yarrow).
A type of peat moss with natural antiseptic properties. Historically used as wound dressings due to its absorbency and mild antibacterial qualities.
A tapered rod rotated against a fireboard to generate friction heat in bow drill and hand drill fire-starting methods.
To join two pieces of rope or cord together by interweaving their strands, creating a permanent connection stronger than a knot.
A rigid or flexible device used to immobilize a fractured or injured body part, preventing further damage during transport.
An injury to a ligament (tissue connecting bones at a joint) caused by stretching or tearing. Graded I (mild) to III (complete tear).
A sudden, violent gust of wind, often accompanied by rain or snow. Squalls can appear with little warning, especially near water.
To make a patient's condition steady and prevent it from worsening. Often the primary goal in field medicine before evacuation.
The part of a rope that is not actively being used to tie a knot — the 'static' end. Opposite of the working end.
To completely destroy all microorganisms on a surface or instrument, typically using heat, chemicals, or radiation. More thorough than disinfecting.
The breastbone — the flat bone in the center of the chest. CPR compressions are performed on the lower half of the sternum.
A simple fire safety technique taught to children and adults to extinguish a fire on their clothes or body.
An injury to a muscle or tendon (tissue connecting muscle to bone) caused by overstretching or tearing. Different from a sprain.
A river hazard where water flows through an obstruction (like fallen trees or rocks) but solid objects (including people) cannot pass through.
The act of drawing fluid out using a vacuum or negative pressure. Used to clear airways, drain wounds, and extract venom (limited effectiveness).
The mental attitude and psychological preparedness that determines whether a person thrives or gives up in a survival situation.
A stitch or series of stitches used to close a wound. In field medicine, improvised sutures can be made from fishing line or thread.
A bitter-tasting organic substance found in bark, leaves, and unripe fruit. Used to tan leather, purify water, and as an astringent for wounds. Must be leached from acorns before eating.
A large sheet of waterproof material (tarpaulin) used for shelter, rain collection, ground cover, and improvised stretchers.
An adjustable loop knot that can be tightened or loosened while under tension. Commonly used for tent guy lines.
A cord of strong tissue connecting a muscle to a bone. Tendons transmit the force of muscle contraction to move bones.
The physical features and characteristics of an area of land — hills, valleys, rivers, forests, rocks. Understanding terrain is essential for navigation.
Navigation by comparing visible terrain features (hills, rivers, ridgelines) to a topographic map to determine position.
A serious bacterial infection that causes painful muscle spasms and can be fatal. Enters through deep puncture wounds or cuts contaminated with soil.
A roof covering made from dried plant materials (grass, reeds, palm leaves) layered to shed rain. One of the oldest roofing methods.
A prickly plant with purple flowers. Despite the spines, the stalks, roots, and young leaves are edible when the spines are removed.
The chest area — the part of the body between the neck and the abdomen, containing the heart and lungs.
A concentrated herbal extract made by soaking plant material in alcohol or vinegar. Used in field medicine for wound treatment.
Ideally dry, fluffy material that ignites easily from a spark or small flame. Examples include dry grass, birch bark, cotton balls, and char cloth.
A short crosspiece (stick, bone, or metal) pushed through a loop to fasten something. Used in improvised closures, snares, and shelters.
A detailed map showing terrain features using contour lines, elevation markings, and symbols for water, vegetation, and human-made structures.
A constricting or compressing device used to control arterial and venous blood flow to a portion of an extremity for a period of time, typically to stop life-threatening bleeding.
The windpipe — the tube carrying air from the throat to the lungs. Choking occurs when the trachea is blocked.
A pulling force applied to a limb or body part to realign bones or relieve muscle spasm. Used in femur fracture management.
A clear plastic bag tied over a leafy branch to collect water. The sun causes the leaves to release moisture, which condenses inside the bag.
The process of determining the priority of patients' treatments based on the severity of their condition.
A stream or river that flows into a larger river. Following tributaries downstream leads to larger water sources and often to civilization.
Relating to the warm, humid region near the equator. Tropical environments have dense vegetation, high rainfall, and unique survival challenges (insects, humidity, fungal infections).
The direction toward the geographic North Pole — the fixed point where Earth's axis of rotation meets its surface. Different from magnetic north.
A thickened underground stem or root of a plant, used as food storage. Examples include cattail roots, Jerusalem artichoke, and wild potato.
The cloudiness or haziness of water caused by suspended particles. High turbidity can harbor pathogens and must be addressed before purification.
A bright yellow root related to ginger, with anti-inflammatory properties. Used in field medicine to reduce swelling and as a mild antiseptic.
Low-growing vegetation beneath the main canopy of a forest. Includes shrubs, ferns, and young trees. Can impede movement.
A systematic, multi-step process for testing an unknown wild plant for toxicity before eating. Takes approximately 24 hours to complete safely.
Universal Transverse Mercator — a coordinate system that divides the Earth into 60 zones and uses meters for precise location description.
Relating to veins — the blood vessels carrying oxygen-depleted blood back to the heart. Venous bleeding is dark red and flows steadily.
Relating to the front or belly side of the body.
Measurable indicators of essential body functions: pulse rate, breathing rate, blood pressure, and temperature. Changes indicate patient deterioration.
A chemical tablet (typically containing iodine or chlorine dioxide) dissolved in water to kill pathogens and make it safe to drink.
An area of land where all water drains to a common outlet point (river, lake, or ocean). Understanding watersheds helps locate water sources.
A building technique using woven wooden strips (wattle) coated with a mixture of mud, clay, and straw (daub). Creates solid walls.
A reference point or set of coordinates that identify a specific location in physical space.
Strong, flat woven fabric (usually nylon) used for straps, belts, and harnesses. Found in backpack straps, seat belts, and rescue equipment.
A stone used for sharpening blades by grinding the edge at the correct angle. Natural whetstones are found in riverbeds.
Tightly wrapping thread or cord around the end of a rope to prevent it from unraveling (fraying).
A weather condition where snow and cloud cover make it impossible to distinguish the ground from the sky. Causes total disorientation.
The bark of willow trees, containing salicin — a natural compound similar to aspirin. Chewed or brewed as tea to reduce pain and fever.
The perceived decrease in air temperature felt by the body due to wind. Moving air strips heat from the body faster than still air.
A barrier (natural or constructed) that blocks or reduces wind speed, significantly reducing wind chill and heat loss.
The side facing into the wind. Exposed to the full force of the wind.
The active end of a rope being used to tie a knot. The end you're manipulating.
A flowering plant (Achillea millefolium) with feathery leaves and white/pink flower clusters. Used in field medicine to help stop bleeding and as an antiseptic poultice.
